# Nested Blocks Example

This example demonstrates how to use nested blocks to create wrapper patterns.

## Simple Wrapper

Instead of using separate blocks at different positions, you can use a wrapper block
that includes another block:

```tangle:///output/wrapped.txt?at=wrapper
=== Header ===
{{include:content}}
=== Footer ===
```

```tangle:///output/wrapped.txt?at=content
This is the main content.
It will be wrapped between the header and footer.
```

## Multiple Inclusions

A wrapper can include multiple blocks:

```tangle:///output/multi.txt?at=wrapper
// File: multi.txt
// Generated by lit

{{include:imports}}

{{include:main}}

{{include:helpers}}
```

```tangle:///output/multi.txt?at=imports
use std::fs;
use std::io::Result;
```

```tangle:///output/multi.txt?at=main
fn main() {
    println!("Hello from nested blocks!");
    helper();
}
```

```tangle:///output/multi.txt?at=helpers
fn helper() {
    println!("Helper function");
}
```

## Deeply Nested

Blocks can nest multiple levels deep:

```tangle:///output/deep.txt?at=outer
Outer layer start
{{include:middle}}
Outer layer end
```

```tangle:///output/deep.txt?at=middle
Middle layer start
{{include:inner}}
Middle layer end
```

```tangle:///output/deep.txt?at=inner
Inner content!
```

## HTML Template Example

Nested blocks are perfect for HTML templates:

```tangle:///output/page.html?at=page
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
{{include:head}}
<body>
{{include:body}}
</body>
</html>
```

```tangle:///output/page.html?at=head
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <title>Nested Blocks Demo</title>
    <style>
        body { font-family: sans-serif; }
    </style>
</head>
```

```tangle:///output/page.html?at=body
<h1>Welcome to Nested Blocks</h1>
<p>This HTML was generated using nested blocks in lit!</p>
{{include:features}}
```

```tangle:///output/page.html?at=features
<ul>
    <li>Clean separation of concerns</li>
    <li>Reusable components</li>
    <li>Hierarchical composition</li>
</ul>
```
